nodes.name: Sticky Note

parameters.content:
## Try it out!

### This workflow takes an image and generates a caption for it using AI. The OpenAI node has been able to do this for a while but this workflow demonstrates how to achieve the same with other multimodal vision models such as Google's Gemini.

Additional, we'll use the Edit Image node to overlay the generated caption onto the image. This can be useful for publications or can be repurposed for copyrights and/or watermarks.

### Need Help?
Join the [Discord]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} or ask in the [Forum]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

nodes.name: Sticky Note1

parameters.content:
## 1. Import an Image 
[Read more about the HTTP request node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

For this demonstration, we'll grab an image off Pexels.com - a popular free stock photography site - by using the HTTP request node to download.

In your own workflows, this can be replaces by other triggers such as webhooks.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Sticky Note2

parameters.content:
## 2. Using Vision Model to Generate Caption
[Learn more about the Basic LLM Chain]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

n8n's basic LLM node supports multimodal input by allowing you to specify either a binary or an image url to send to a compatible LLM. This makes it easy to start utilising this powerful feature for visual classification or OCR tasks which have previously depended on more dedicated OCR models.

Here, we've simply passed our image binary as a "user message" option, asking the LLM to help us generate a caption title and text which is appropriate for the given subject. Once generated, we'll pass this text along with the image to combine them both.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Sticky Note3

parameters.content:
## 3. Overlay Caption on Image 
[Read more about the Edit Image node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

Finally, we’ll perform some basic calculations to place the generated caption onto the image. With n8n's user-friendly image editing features, this can be done entirely within the workflow!

The Code node tool is ideal for these types of calculations and is used here to position the caption at the bottom of the image. To create the overlay, the Edit Image node enables us to insert text onto the image, which we’ll use to add the generated caption.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

nodes.name: Image Captioning Agent

parameters.text: Generate a caption for this image.

messageValues.message:
=You role is to provide an appropriate image caption for user provided images.

The individual components of a caption are as follows: who, when, where, context and miscellaneous. For a really good caption, follow this template: who + when + where + context + miscellaneous

Give the caption a punny title.

parameters.content:
## Try it out!

### This workflow takes an image and generates a caption for it using AI. The OpenAI node has been able to do this for a while but this workflow demonstrates how to achieve the same with other multimodal vision models such as Google's Gemini.

Additional, we'll use the Edit Image node to overlay the generated caption onto the image. This can be useful for publications or can be repurposed for copyrights and/or watermarks.

### Need Help?
Join the [Discord]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} or ask in the [Forum]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

## 1. Import an Image 
[Read more about the HTTP request node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

For this demonstration, we'll grab an image off Pexels.com - a popular free stock photography site - by using the HTTP request node to download.

In your own workflows, this can be replaces by other triggers such as webhooks.

## 2. Using Vision Model to Generate Caption
[Learn more about the Basic LLM Chain]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

n8n's basic LLM node supports multimodal input by allowing you to specify either a binary or an image url to send to a compatible LLM. This makes it easy to start utilising this powerful feature for visual classification or OCR tasks which have previously depended on more dedicated OCR models.

Here, we've simply passed our image binary as a "user message" option, asking the LLM to help us generate a caption title and text which is appropriate for the given subject. Once generated, we'll pass this text along with the image to combine them both.

## 3. Overlay Caption on Image 
[Read more about the Edit Image node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

Finally, we’ll perform some basic calculations to place the generated caption onto the image. With n8n's user-friendly image editing features, this can be done entirely within the workflow!

The Code node tool is ideal for these types of calculations and is used here to position the caption at the bottom of the image. To create the overlay, the Edit Image node enables us to insert text onto the image, which we’ll use to add the generated caption.

**FIG 1.** Example input image with AI generated caption
![Example Output]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

prerequisites: An active n8n instance installed locally, in a Docker container, or hosted on n8n Cloud.
Basic familiarity with importing JSON templates in n8n.
setupInstructions: Download the JSON configuration for this workflow.
Ensure you set up credentials for all connected integrations.
Test your trigger and run execution.
requiredCredentials: google Palm  A P I details
configurationSteps: Verify the settings of the trigger node "When clicking ‘Test workflow’".
Open the "Google Gemini Chat Model" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Structured Output Parser" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Get Info" node (n8n-nodes-base.editImage) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Resize For AI" node (n8n-nodes-base.editImage) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Apply Caption to Image" node (n8n-nodes-base.editImage) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Merge Image & Caption" node (n8n-nodes-base.merge) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Merge Caption & Positions" node (n8n-nodes-base.merge) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Get Image" node (n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note1" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note2" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note3" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note4" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Image Captioning Agent" node (n8n-nodes-base.noOp) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
No global variables node (Set node) is present. If you need dynamic constants, add a Set node after the trigger.
testingSteps: Click the "Test step" or "Listen for test event" button on your Trigger node.
Send a test trigger from your source service (e.g. fill a test form or trigger a webhook trigger).
Verify that the node canvas lights up green, indicating successful data flow.
Open the final destination node to confirm that output records were saved correctly.
instructions.expectedResult: Upon trigger activation, inputs will flow through the configured nodes and execute actions successfully.
troubleshooting: Check credential warning symbols. Most workflow issues stem from expired OAuth connections or missing API permissions.
Verify that the data keys coming from your Trigger node match the exact names mapped in subsequent nodes (e.g., {{ $json.body.email }}).
Open the Execution history tab in n8n to view logs and determine which node failed.
securityNotes: Never hardcode API keys, passwords, or authentication bearer tokens in the plain text fields of node parameters.
If you download or share this workflow JSON, sanitize any sensitive parameters or configuration nodes.
customizationIdeas: Add an email node at the end to notify you of successful executions.
Incorporate an AI LLM node to summarize trigger payloads before writing them to integrations.
